coredata.dc:title: Rational synthesis of carbon dots with phosphate ester group for direct mapping of endogenous alkaline phosphatase and polarity monitoring in living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xing, Yanzhi
Xu, Yulong
Lin, Yanna
Shuang, E
Leng, Han
Wang, Jianhua
Chen, Xuwei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively employed in biomolecule imaging. However, the imaging of biological enzymes with CDs has not been reported, which greatly limits their application in biological imaging. Herein, for the first time, a new type of fluorescent CDs is elaborately designed to realize the direct mapping of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in cells. The obtained phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped CDs (P, N-CDs) generate specific structures including xanthene oxide and phosphate ester, thereby enabling P, N-CDs to be exclusively cleaved by ALP without auxiliary media. The fluorescence intensity of P, N-CDs can be specifically turned on in the presence of ALP, making them powerful probes for sensitive sensing of ALP activity with a detection limit of 1.27 U·L− 1. Meanwhile, P, N-CDs possessing electron deficiency structure fulfill sensitive responding to polarity variations. The excellent photo-bleaching resistance and biocompatibility of the P, N-CDs are taken for directly mapping the intracellular endogenous ALP via turned-on fluorescence imaging, as well as real-time monitoring the polarity fluctuation in cells through ratiometric fluorescence imaging. The present work offers a new way to design and synthesize functional CDs for direct imaging of intracellular enzymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: P, N-CDs
Dual function
Alkaline phosphatase
Polarity
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of ALP with P, N-CDs
Fluorescence imaging of ALP in cells
Fluorescence monitoring of intracellular polarity variations
Design and synthesis of P, N-CDs
Fluorescence response of P, N-CDs to solvent polarity
Highly selective sensing of ALP activity with P, N-CDs
Cytotoxicity, cell uptake kinetics and cellular uptake pathways
Fluorescence mapping of endogenous intracellular ALP in live cells.
Ratiometric fluorescence assay of intracellular polarity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), FT-IR spectrum (B), XRD pattern (C) and high-resolution XPS spectra (D), C 1s spectra (E), N 1s spectra (F), P 2p spectra (G) and O 1s spectra of P, N-CDs (H).
UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of P, N-CDs (A). Fluorescence spectra of P, N-CDs under different excitations (B). Lifetime decay profile of P, N-CDs (C).

Fluorescence spectra of P, N-CDs in different solvents (A). Fluorescence spectra of P, N-CDs in the solvent mixtures of H2O and 1,4-dioxane (B). Plot fit of I 590/I 530 against
Δ
f (C). The CIE diagram of P, N-CDs with the changes of polarity (D). Fluorescence color of P, N-CDs with the changes of polarity under daylight and UV irradiation (E).

Fluorescence response of P, N-CDs to ALP of different concentration (A). The linear relationship of (F-F0)/F0 towards ALP concentration, Error bars are estimated from triplicate measurements (B). Effect of different potential interference on the fluorescence intensity of P, N-CDs (C).

Fluorescence images of P, N-CDs in HeLa cells and HEK 293 cells (A). Immunofluorescence analysis with an anti-ALP antibody and a secondary fluorescent antibody (goat anti-rabbit IgG (H + L) Fluor594-conjugated) (B). Scale bar of 20 μm.
Fluorescence images of HeLa cells co-incubated with commercial organelle trackers and P, N-CDs. Scale bar: 20 μm.

Confocal fluorescence images of LX-2 and HepG2 cells stained with P, N-CDs (A). Fluorescence ratio of I red/I green in different cells (B). Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells with P, N-CDs after the addition of DMSO (C). Fluorescence ratio of I red/I green after DMSO treatment in HepG2 cells (D). Insets in (B) and (D): pseudo-colored ratiometric images.
Schematic illustration for the synthetic route of P, N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Rational synthesis of carbon dots with phosphate ester group for direct mapping of endogenous alkaline phosphatase and polarity monitoring in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization of Ministry of Education, Dalian Minzu University, Dalian 116600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization of Ministry of Education, Dalian Minzu University, Dalian 116600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively employed in biomolecule imaging. However, the imaging of biological enzymes with CDs has not been reported, which greatly limits their application in biological imaging. Herein, for the first time, a new type of fluorescent CDs is elaborately designed to realize the direct mapping of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) in cells. The obtained phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped CDs (P, N-CDs) generate specific structures including xanthene oxide and phosphate ester, thereby enabling P, N-CDs to be exclusively cleaved by ALP without auxiliary media. The fluorescence intensity of P, N-CDs can be specifically turned on in the presence of ALP, making them powerful probes for sensitive sensing of ALP activity with a detection limit of 1.27 U·L− 1. Meanwhile, P, N-CDs possessing electron deficiency structure fulfill sensitive responding to polarity variations. The excellent photo-bleaching resistance and biocompatibility of the P, N-CDs are taken for directly mapping the intracellular endogenous ALP via turned-on fluorescence imaging, as well as real-time monitoring the polarity fluctuation in cells through ratiometric fluorescence imaging. The present work offers a new way to design and synthesize functional CDs for direct imaging of intracellular enzymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) mainly holds a position as catalyst for biological matrix dephosphorization from protein and non-protein substrates. As a typical biomarker, ALP is widely distributed in organisms and actively gets involved in essential physiological processes, e.g., signal transduction, molecular transport, metabolism and the expression of the genetic [1,2]. ALP also plays a part in many pathological procedures. Nowadays mounting evidence implies the correlation between the abnormal level of ALP and various diseases such as cancer, diabetes, heart disease, bone disorders and liver dysfunction [3–5], while the way of ALP involved in these pathological function remains unclear, therefore, it is of great significance to develop highly selective and accurate ALP assay strategy to achieve its visual monitoring, so as to unveil the mechanism of ALP activities in bio-systems, and to offer basis for clinical diagnosis.

Intracellular polarity is a key indicator of bio-systems. Cell status and regulation mechanism go hand in hand with polarity, since extensive organic and inorganic reaction processes are subject to the surrounding polarity [6,7]. A series of cellular processes involved in spatial arrangement and protein composition (e.g. apoptosis, differentiation, membrane rearrangement, activation of the immune response, directional cell migration, and vectorial transport of molecules across cell layers) might lead to the fluctuations of intracellular polarity [8]. Many physiological disorders and pathological diseases, such as diabetes and Alzheimer’s disease, have been certificated to be fed back by the abnormal level of intracellular polarity as well [9,10]. Therefore, the real-time monitoring of intracellular polarity is quite beneficial to the early clinical diagnosis.

Up to now, various techniques including colorimetry [11,12], chromatography [13], surface enhanced resonance Raman scattering [14] and electrochemical methods [15] have been developed for quantitative ALP assay. Nonetheless, tedious sample preparation procedures are usually needed to match the demand on detection selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, their applications in detecting biological systems in vivo are quite difficult [16,17]. In contrast, fluorescent spectrometry has attracted considerable attention due to its excellent spatial-temporal resolution and fast response, which meet the growing demand for high-efficiency detection and real-time imaging of ALP [18,19]. By coincidence, fluorescence spectrometry also provides a powerful tool for polarity monitoring. Recently, many fluorescent materials such as organic dyes [20,21], metal nanoclusters [22,23], organic frameworks (MOFs/COFs) [24,25] and semiconductor quantum dots [26] have attracted increasing attention in such fields. However, the practical applications still suffer from specialized synthetic skills and complicated modification procedures.

Zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs), as a fascinating class of excellent fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted widespread attention due to their low cost, less toxicity, high photostability, outstanding biocompatibility and favorable photoluminescent (PL) behaviors in the last two decades [27–30]. With these distinct benefits, CDs are regarded as a potential tool to visualize the distribution and monitor the dynamic changes of substances in living cells. However, the practical applications of CDs in phosphatase are scanty, due to the fact that ALP-catalytic products are linked with the fluorescence quencher of CDs, and the faint fluorescence is not favorable for the visual monitoring of ALP in organisms. In recent years some efforts have been taken to implement the ALP assay with CDs, while the developed fluorescence off-on mode is a little complicated as delicate condition modulation is required, and the examples of CDs based fluorescent probes still lack the ability to directly and accurately detect protein [31,32]. Theoretically, the physico-chemical features and optical properties of CDs could be extensively tunable by modulating precursors and/or manipulating reaction conditions in the preparation of CDs [33]. Heteroatom doping generally refers to incorporating atomic impurities into carbon cores, which has been proven to be an effective strategy to enhance the performance of CDs and provide available sites for target sensing [34,35]. Nitrogen can provide extra electrons, leading to the upward shift of Fermi level and the change of optical properties [36,37]. Phosphorus, as an n-type donor, forms substitution defects in carbon clusters [38]. Therefore, N and P doped CDs usually exhibit higher quantum yield and longer emission, and own more active sites than normal CDs, and even introduce new and unexpected properties [39,40], making N and P doped CDs favorable candidates for biological sensing.

In present study, we developed bi-functional CDs via rational design. m-aminophenol and phytic acid are adopted as precursors to synthesize ALP-activatable and polarity-responsive P, N-CDs through a simple hydrothermal route. The phosphate groups on the carbon skeleton of the as-synthesized CDs serve as the recognizing unit for ALP activity. Triggered by ALP enzymatic reaction, P, N-CDs will release phosphate radicals and induce significant fluorescence enhancement. At the same time, P, N-CDs display blue-shift in fluorescence emission upon the increased environmental polarity, due to the fact that the formation of imino groups provides P, N-CDs with electrophilic surface, which enhances the charges transfer of P, N-CDs and the response of P, N-CDs to polarity. The sensitive sensing of ALP and polarity not only enable P, N-CDs as a powerful probe for quantitative assay, but also make it possible to achieve the direct imaging of endogenous ALP in different organelles (mitochondria, endoplasmic reticula, lysosomes) via fluorescence turn-on mode for reliable mapping of the distribution of ALP in living cells, and to distinguish the polarity divergence in different cellular environments.

Phytic acid and m-aminophenol were purchased from McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Amino acids, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, AlCl3, FeCl3·6H2O and FeCl2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). 3-(4,-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliumbromide (MTT) assay kit was provided by Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). 1,4-dioxane, ethylacetate, tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone, ethanol, methanol, 2-deoxy-d-glucose, chlorpromazine, genistein, methyl beta-cyclodextrin and amiloride were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Alkaline phosphatase antibody and goat anti-rabbit IgG (H + L) Fluor594-conjugated were purchased from Affinity Biologicals Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). ER-Tracker Red, Lyso-Tracker Red and Mito-Tracker Red were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cells were provided by Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (resistivity > 18 MΩ·cm).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed on a JEM-ARM200F field-emission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) were conducted on a MultiMode3D atomic force microscopy (Veeco, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a VERTEX70 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were obtained on an ESCALAB 250Xi analysis system (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA). Photoluminescence behaviors were measured by a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) under an optical path length of 1.0 cm. Cytotoxicity assay was conducted on a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader (Biotek Instruments, Inc, Germany). Cell images were taken on a widefield fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan) and FV-1200 confocal fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan). Zeta potential analyses were performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, England).

Typically, 0.34 mg m-aminophenol, 1.05 mL phytic acid (70%, v/v) and 20 mL deionized water were sealed into a 50-mL Teflon autoclave and kept for reaction at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant solution was filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities, and then dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (3500 Da) for 48 h to remove unreacted components. The collected solution after dialysis was lyophilized at −40 °C. The obtained products were named P, N-CDs and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of ALP with P, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorescent assay of ALP, 2.0 mL of P, N-CDs (50 μg·mL−1, prepared in 10 mM PBS buffer of pH 7.40) was mixed with 0.05 mL ALP solution (0–400 U·L−1), and the resultant mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. The fluorescence spectra of P, N-CDs before and after the addition of ALP were recorded under an excitation of 405 nm. The calibration curve was achieved by plotting ALP content against the fluorescence change ((F0-F)/F0), where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity at 530 nm in the absence and presence of ALP respectively.
The assay selectivity was investigated by adding potential interfering species into the reaction system under the same conditions. All measurements were conducted in triplicate.

HeLa cells were cultured in high glucose medium DMEM supplemented with 10% (v/v) heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin antibiotics at 37 °C under 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. Before cell experiments, the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared P, N-CDs was assessed by MTT assay. Cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and treated with different concentrations of P, N-CDs in 100 μL serum-free DMEM medium for 24 h. Subsequently, the waste medium was replaced by fresh one, and 10 μL of MTT (5 mg·mL−1) was added into each well and kept incubation for 4 h. After adding 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide, the plate was vibrated for 5 min and the absorbance at 570 nm was recorded.

For internalization pathways investigation, different pathway inhibitors including 2-deoxy-d-glucose (30 mM, ATP synthesis inhibitor), chlorpromazine (10 μM, clathrin-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), genistein (200 μM, caveolae-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), methyl beta-cyclodextrin (2 mM, lipid raft mediated endocytosis inhibitor) and amiloride (100 μM, large cell phagocytosis inhibitor) were pre-cultured with HeLa cells at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, the culture liquid was replaced with P, N-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 30 min. Finally, 60× objective lens (Ex/Em = 405 nm/500–540 nm) was used for fluorescence imaging on confocal fluorescence microscope. As for assays of passive diffusion, HeLa cells were fixed with paraformaldehyde for 10 min, then P, N-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) were added and co-incubated for 5, 15 and 30 min respectively. The optical density (OPD) of P, N-CDs in cells was evaluated by Image J image-processing techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of ALP in cells
ce:section.ce:para: HEK 293 cells and HeLa cells were grown in 20-mm dishes at 37 °C for 24 h, and then incubated with P, N-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 30 min, followed by washing thoroughly with PBS to remove residual P, N-CDs in culture medium. The cells were then subjected to fluorescence imaging using 60× oil immersion objective lens under excited by a 405 nm laser. The emission ranging from 500 nm to 540 nm were recorded as the green-channel imaging result with the confocal laser scanning microscope (Olympus, Japan).

For inhibition experiments, commercial phosphatase inhibitor cocktail was diluted 100 times and pre-incubated with the cells for 12 h. P, N-CDs solution was then added and further incubated with the mixture for 30 min before cell imaging.

To monitor the distribution of ALP in cells, HeLa cells were pretreated with P, N-CDs (50 μg·mL−1) for 30 min and washed three times with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.40). Then, the cells were incubated with commercial organelle probes including Mito-Tracker Red, ER-Tracker Red or Lyso-Tracker Red. The confocal images were performed on Olympus laser confocal fluorescence microscope using the sequential frame scanning mode with 60× objective lens. All images were acquired with the same settings for each channel. The microscopy laser was set using 405 nm and 559 nm as the excitation wavelength, 500–540 nm as the emission wavelength of green channel and 580–630 nm as the emission wavelength of red channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence monitoring of intracellular polarity variations
ce:section.ce:para: Different adherent cell lines (HepG2 and LX-2 cells) were incubated with 50 μg·mL− 1 P, N-CDs for 30 min, then the cells were washed with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.40) for three times. Fluorescence images were acquired using a 60× objective lens by a confocal fluorescence microscope. The microscopy laser was set as follows: Ex/Em = 405/500–540 nm, 570–630 nm. The pixel intensity in each fluorescence image was measured via Image J software and averaged from at least three cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthesis of P, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that most of organic probes for ALP detection are composed of xanthene (as the luminescence center) and phosphate groups (as the reactive site) [21,41,42]. Xanthene could be produced from m-aminophenol derivatives and acid derivatives via Friedel-Crafts reaction under the catalysis of Lewis acid. The produced fluorophore would continue to polymerize and carbonize to form polymer networks and carbon nuclei [43]. In present study, m-aminophenol and phytic acid were adopted as precursors for the synthesis of P, N-CDs. Phytic acid consists of carbon skeleton and six phosphate groups, which could be decomposed into phosphoric acid and inositol fragments under high pressure and high temperature. The synthesis process of P, N-CDs was shown in Scheme 1 . The introduction of phytic acid not only acted as catalyst for Friedel-Crafts reaction, but also served itself as a precursor to react with m-aminophenol to form fluorophore xanthene. Subsequently, the polymerization of intermediate under high temperature and the presence of inositol lead to the formation of a large amount of –OH on the surface of the carbon net. With the proceeding of the preparation, the intermediate products continued to react with phytic acid fragments by condensation reactions to form the final product of P, N-CDs.

TEM revealed that the obtained P, N-CDs were quasi-spherical and homogeneously dispersed (Fig. 1
A). The average particle size was 5.23 nm with relatively narrow particle size distribution between 2.92 nm and 7.09 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed the crystal phase of P, N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 B, the diffraction peaks centered at 26.0° and 46.7° corresponded to the (002) and (100) diffraction of disordered carbonaceous structure of graphite C, suggesting that the as-synthesized P, N-CDs owned amorphous graphite structure. FT-IR spectra verified that there were mainly carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, diphenyl ether and aromatic hydrocarbon groups included in the obtained CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 C, the characteristic absorption bands of stretching vibration of COC stretching vibration of diphenyl ether groups in xanthene was at 1050 cm−1. The CN bonds of imine could be observed at 1641 cm−1. The CN and NH bonds were observed in 2364 cm−1 and 2908 cm−1. The strong bands at 3411 cm−1 were attributed to the OH stretching vibrations. It is worth noting that there were abundant phosphorus-oxygen groups on the surface of CDs. The bands centered at 528 cm−1 and 1139 cm−1 represented PO and PO stretching frequencies, respectively. Moreover, the adsorption peak assigned to POC(aromatic) stretching vibration appeared at 854 cm−1, suggesting that the phosphate oxygen group was attached to the aromatic ring. The detailed bonding configuration and chemical composition were further analyzed by XPS analysis, with results shown in Fig. 1 D. XPS results indicated that there were C, P, N, and O elements in the obtained P, N-CDs. In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 E), the peaks at binding energy of 284.2 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.9 eV were respectively ascribed to CC, CP, CN, CO, and CO, indicating that P and N atoms are indeed doped into the P, N-CDs. The high-resolution N 1 s spectra (Fig. 1 F) at binding energies of 399.8 and 400.4 eV could be easily identified, which were attributed to the formation of CNC and CN. The high-resolution P 2p spectrum (Fig. 1 G) of P, N-CDs was collected to penetrate more into fine structure of phosphorus. The peak at 133.6 eV corresponded to C-OP(OH)2, in which P atom was bonded to one C and three O atoms. The peak at 135.0 eV went with COP type groups, such as (CO)PO3. Besides, the binding energy of 133.1 eV in P 2p spectrum, the binding energies of 531.7 eV and 532.5 eV in O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1 H) were attributed to the formed PO and COP. XPS results well suggested that P element in the obtained P, N-CDs existed mainly in the form of phosphate esters. The surface phosphate groups were further determined employing mixed-indicator titration method, demonstrating that the content of phosphomonoester in P, N-CDs was 0.41 μmol·mg−1. The chemical bonds deduced from FT-IR spectra were consistent to the results of high-resolution XPS scan, suggesting that the as-prepared P, N-CDs conformed to the expected configuration.

The optical properties of the as-synthesized P, N-CDs were systematically investigated. As shown in Fig. 2
A, the intense peak around 290 nm in the UV–vis spectrum was ascribed to the π–π* transitions of CC, CN or NP groups, and the weak band centered around 440 nm suggested an extended conjugated structure caused by aromatic rings [44]. Fig. 2 B showed the fluorescence behavior of P, N-CDs under different excitations. The as-synthesized P, N-CDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior when the excitation wavelength changed from 340 nm to 450 nm, and displayed an emission maximum at 530 nm with green fluorescence under 400 nm excitation, along with an average lifetime of 7.48 ns (Fig. 2 C) by fitting the fluorescence decay profile with a dual-exponential curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of P, N-CDs to solvent polarity
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3
A illustrated the fluorescence spectra of P, N-CDs in solvents of different polarities ( Table S1 ). It could be seen that the emission maximum of P, N-CDs displayed polarity-dependent behavior. With various solvents covering a wide range of solvent polarity (
Δ
f) from
Δ
f = 0.021 (1,4-dioxane) to
Δ
f = 0.321 (water), a blue-shift of emission maximum occurred with increased polarity. When
Δ
f < 0.285, the fluorescence wavelength showed a slight response to the changes of polarity, while
Δ
f > 0.285 the emissions shift was obvious.

1,4-dioxane can best simulate bio-membranes [45], therefore, the fluorescence response of P, N-CDs to solvent polarity was further studied by investigating their fluorescence behavior in mixtures of 1,4-dioxane and water ( Table S2 ). As illustrated in Fig. 3 B, the increasing polarity induced by the water fraction in the mixture caused obvious blue-shift of P, N-CDs emission maximum, i.e., from 590 nm in the 1,4-dioxane to 530 nm in water. The fluorescence ratio of I 590/I 530 (I 590 and I 530 respectively represented the fluorescence intensity of P, N-CDs at 590 nm and 530 nm) and showed good linearity against
Δ
f in the range of 20–90 vol%, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.995 (Fig. 3 C, inset: the linear equation is I 590/I 530 = -17.15
Δ
f + 6.451), suggesting that the obtained P, N-CDs could be explored as efficient solvated fluorometric discoloration probes for polarity determination or assay of water content in different solvents. CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 3 D) well provided standard observations and marked the obvious fluorescence color change. At the same time, the luminescence of P, N-CDs turned from red to green under UV light, providing a more intuitive difference in fluorescence emission which could be distinguished by the naked eye (Fig. 3 E). The reason for the shift of maximum emission of the as-synthesized P, N-CDs might be that the formation of abundant imine nitrogen on the surface introduced electron deficiency into the P, N-CDs. Under irradiation the charges in P, N-CDs were rearranged, and the transfer from the electron-donating groups to the electron-withdrawing groups was dramatically improved. Thus, the stable excited states of P, N-CDs were easily affected by the surrounding environment, the sensitivity of P, N-CDs responding to polarity was significantly improved, resulting in the change of fluorescence spectrum [46].

To prove the feasibility to explore P, N-CDs as probes for polarity monitoring, the sensing selectivity of P, N-CDs was investigated in water/1,4-dioxane (50%/50%) mixture over various coexisting species including different ions, amino acids and proteins. Fig. S1 revealed that the presence of the above substances nearly posed no influence on the response of P, N-CDs, indicating that P, N-CDs exhibited favorable anti-interference performance. The photoluminescence of P, N-CDs at 530 nm and 590 nm exhibited no obvious change under the continuous irradiation with a xenon lamp for 2 h, suggesting that the P, N-CDs possessed good photo-bleaching resistance and could be used for long-term monitoring ( Fig. S2 ). The photoluminescence of P, N-CDs remained quite stable at pH 4.0–9.0 (Fig. S3 A) and exhibited favorable tolerance to high ionic strength (Fig. S3 B). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of P, N-CDs was almost unchanged after being stored at room temperature for 180 days (Fig. S3 C). These results clearly suggested that P, N-CDs own great potential as polarity probes in dealing with complex biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly selective sensing of ALP activity with P, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As the surface of the as-prepared P, N-CDs was abundant with phosphate ester groups, thus their potential applications in ALP sensing were further investigated. Fig. 4
A showed the fluorescence spectra of P, N-CDs in the presence/absence of ALP. It could be seen that the presence of ALP induced significant fluorescence enhancement of P, N-CDs, suggesting the potential of P, N-CDs as nanoprobes for ALP sensing in turn-on mode. The fluorescence enhancement efficiency (F-F0)/F0 (F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of P, N-CDs at 530 nm before/after the addition of ALP) exhibited a good linear relationship with ALP activity in the range of 5–300 U·L− 1 (Fig. 4 B). The detection limit was deduced to be 1.27 U·L− 1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is superior to that reported ALP sensing systems, as summarized in Table S3 .

The complexity of real samples offers great challenges in practical ALP detection. In order to assess the anti-interference ability of P, N-CDs, the typical substances in biological systems were used as potential interference sources (including metal ions, anions, amino acids and proteins) to test the fluorescence response of P, N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 C, significant change of fluorescence intensity was observed for ALP only, and the influence of other species on P, N-CDs fluorescence was negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity, cell uptake kinetics and cellular uptake pathways
ce:section.ce:para:
MTT assay was performed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of P, N-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S8
, 85% cells remained alive even when the P, N-CDs concentration was high up to 250 μg·mL−1, suggesting the favorable biocompatibility of the as-synthetized P, N-CDs and their potential as promising candidates for intracellular sensing. The cellular uptake kinetics of the P, N-CDs were thus investigated by detecting the amount of internalized P, N-CDs under different incubation times. As shown in Fig. S9 , P, N-CDs could readily cross HeLa cell membrane and provided bright fluorescent images within 20 min. Time-course assay indicated that the optical density of P, N-CDs kept stable within 60 min, indicating that P, N-CDs could serve as good cell-imaging probes for long-time monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence mapping of endogenous intracellular ALP in live cells.
ce:section.ce:para:
Encouraged by excellent biocompatibility, P, N-CDs were further used for monitoring the endogenous ALP levels in living cells. HEK 293 (ALP-negative) and HeLa cells (ALP-positive) were selected as models for imaging of ALP viability, because the overexpression of ALP in HeLa cells was compared to normal cells [41]. As could be seen in Fig. 5
A, HeLa cells exhibited strong green fluorescence, whereas relatively weak fluorescence was observed in HEK 293 cells. It was interesting that nearly no fluorescence was observed for both of the cell lines, when they were pre-treated by commercial ALP express inhibitor cocktail I [51]. These well indicated that the appearance of fluorescence in these two cell lines was indeed contributed by endogenous ALP. Moreover, immunofluorescence assays illustrated that the Pearson coefficient of co-localization was high to 91%, suggesting the precise location of the P, N-CDs near to enzyme active site and their great potential as excellent ALP imaging probes (Fig. 5 B).

The subcellular distribution of P, N-CDs was further investigated by co-staining with P, N-CDs and the organelle probes Lyso-Tracker Red, Mito-Tracker Red, and ER-Tracker Red. The colocalization experimental results showed that there was a degree of overlap between P, N-CDs and the above three commercial trackers, indicating the fluorescence signals of P, N-CDs were distributed over the whole cytoplasm (Fig. 6 ). The calculated co-localization correlation coefficients corresponding to mitochondrion, endoplasmic reticulum and lysosome were 0.80, 0.87 and 0.75, respectively. It might be due to the fact that ALP could be expressed in most organelles, thus ALP activities were presented across most cell spaces [52,53]. This phenomenon indicated that the P, N-CDs were not gathered in a certain region, but only concentrated at the location where ALP exists, showing specific relationship between P, N-CDs with ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay of intracellular polarity
ce:section.ce:para:
The different microenvironments between normal cells and cancer cells have attracted attention in recent years. The feasibility of the as-prepared P, N-CDs as ratiometric imaging probes to assay intracellular polarity differences among cancer and normal cells was evaluated via confocal fluorescence imaging assay. Normal human liver cells (LX-2 cells) and corresponding cancer cells (HepG2 cells) were incubated with P, N-CDs for 30 min, CLSM images were captured in green and red channels. As mentioned above, the hypsochromic behavior of P, N-CDs occurred with the decrease of solvent polarity, therefore the green and red region in CLSM images stood for high and low polarity respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 7
A and B, cancer cells exhibited much higher fluorescence ratio (I red /I green) than normal cells, indicating the contribution of red emission was much higher than green emission. The result was consistent with the fact that the polarity of cancer cells was lower than that of normal cells [54,55]. The obvious difference in fluorescence ratio of P, N-CDs offers the possibility to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells.

To further verify the ability of P, N-CDs in monitoring the polarity fluctuation in living cell, certain amount of DMSO was added into the cell culture fluid after the incubation of HepG2 cells with P, N-CDs, as DMSO is low polarity solvent and able to decrease the intracellular polarity [56]. As illustrated in Fig. 7 C and D, clear red fluorescence and weak green fluorescence were observed in HepG2 cells treated with DMSO, and the color of the merged image was reddish orange while that of untreated cells significantly tended to be yellowish. The capability of P, N-CDs in measuring the polarity of living cells by ratio fluorescence imaging powerfully furnished evidence to the feasibility of using P, N-CDs to real-time monitor intracellular polarity.

In summary, bi-functional P, N-CDs was rationally designed and synthesized by using phytic acid and m-aminophenol as precursors through a one-step hydrothermal procedure. The obtained CDs own a similar structure to traditional ALP probes consisting of xanthene structure as luminescence centers and phosphate etser group as recognition site. The enzyme-induced dephosphorylation process endows the as-synthesized CDs sensitive response to ALP, achieving the direct mapping the intracellular endogenous ALP via turned-on fluorescence imaging. At the same time, the introduced electron deficiency followed by the exposure of imine nitrogen on the surface of the P, N-CDs offer them with spectral response to polarity, making them powerful probes for real-time monitoring the polarity fluctuation in cells through ratiometric fluorescence imaging, which provide the practicable feasibility to differentiate cancer cells from normal cells. The present research provides not only bi-functional probes to monitor cell activities, but also a new strategy to design and fabrication of functional CDs for targeted applications.

Yanzhi Xing: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yanna Lin: Data analysis. Shuang E: Data analysis. Yulong Xu: Software. Han Leng: Data curation. Jianhua Wang: Supervision. Xuwei Chen: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Liaoning Science and Technology Development Foundation Guided by Central Government (2022JH6/100100024), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.02.133.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
